comments:
Summary and strengths:
This paper applies an encoder-decoder LSTM architecture with attention to the task of generating news headlines from article text (Gigaword). The authors evaluate two attention variants: a standard “complex” (dot-product) attention and a proposed “simple” attention that explicitly splits hidden units for attention-weight computation versus context representation. They report that the simplified attention improves held-out metrics, analyze unit activations to show that neurons capture linguistic phenomena (subjects, objects, names, negation, etc.), and provide qualitative examples and discussion of typical error modes. The work is clearly motivated, uses a very large dataset, and offers useful empirical and interpretive contributions: (1) demonstrating seq2seq effectiveness on headline generation and (2) proposing an interpretable attention variant with neuron-level analysis.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Evaluation is limited. BLEU is computed on only 384 held-out examples (for efficiency) and the paper lacks broader automatic evaluation (e.g., ROUGE) or human judgments. There is also little statistical analysis (no significance testing) and no per-source breakdown beyond a few anecdotal examples.
- Baselines are weak or missing. The paper compares two internal variants but does not quantitatively compare to prior work or simple baselines (e.g., extractive baselines, lead-3, or the non-RNN attention model referenced). Without these, it is hard to judge practical gains.
- Some methodological details need clarification: treatment of out-of-vocabulary words beyond truncating to 40k, exact beam-search settings used in reported metrics (they note sensitivity to beam size), and how/when scheduled sampling was applied (they say 10% of the time but do not ablate this). The claim that training loss is higher than holdout loss because of teacher forcing differences is plausible but deserves clearer explanation.
- Dataset noise is large (erroneous/coded headlines), and while the paper notes this, it does not quantitatively analyze how much noise affects metrics nor attempts systematic filtering or robustness experiments.
- The novelty is limited: the main architectural pieces are standard; the simple attention split is a modest architectural tweak. The interpretability analysis is interesting but somewhat preliminary and limited by the shallow exploration acknowledged by the authors.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Provide fuller automatic evaluation on the entire holdout set (or a substantial random sample) and report multiple metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) and statistical confidence intervals. Include human evaluation for fluency and faithfulness.
- Compare to strong baselines, including extractive summarization baselines and the previously published non-RNN attention-based headline generation model mentioned in the text.
- Ablation studies: effect of attention split size, number of attention units, scheduled sampling rate, bidirectional encoder, beam width, and use of dropout/regularization.
- Quantify the impact of noisy/coded headlines; either filter such examples and report results or show robustness experiments.
- Expand the interpretability analysis with more systematic probes and examples; release annotated examples and code for reproducibility if possible.

Verdict:
This is a solid empirical paper that applies established sequence-to-sequence techniques to a practical summarization task and contributes a simple, more interpretable attention variant plus neuron-level analysis. It would benefit from stronger, more comprehensive evaluation and baseline comparisons. I recommend publication (or archiving) after addressing evaluation and comparison concerns.
